title: Håfa Adai! Greetings
subtitle: Say hello the Chamorro way
description: Learn how to greet people, say goodbye, and introduce yourself in Chamorro. Greetings are the heart of Chamorro culture — the phrase 'Håfa Adai' is the most iconic expression of welcome from Guam.
grammar_note: In Chamorro, 'Håfa adai' literally means 'What is it?' but is used as a warm greeting meaning 'Hello' or 'Welcome.' It's the most famous Chamorro phrase and appears on Guam license plates.
cultural_note: The Chamorro people of Guam are known for their warm hospitality, called 'inafa'maolek' — a spirit of mutual support and taking care of one another. A proper greeting is the first step in showing respect.

title: Numbers: Håcha to Bente
subtitle: Count from 1 to 20 in Chamorro
description: Chamorro has its own native number system. Learn to count from one to twenty and you'll be able to talk about prices, ages, quantities, and time.
grammar_note: Numbers 1–10 are native Chamorro words. Numbers 11–15 use a hybrid system (ånse, dos, trese, katotse, kinse) borrowed from Spanish. Numbers 16+ follow a Spanish-style 'dies i [number]' pattern, meaning 'ten and [number].' Twenty is 'bente' from Spanish.
cultural_note: Spanish colonial rule (1668–1898) deeply influenced Chamorro language, especially numbers, calendar months, and many everyday words. This blending makes Chamorro unique among Pacific Island languages.

title: Colors: Kulot
subtitle: Describe the world around you
description: Learn the Chamorro words for colors. You'll use these to describe the beautiful blue ocean, the green jungle, and everything in between on the island.
grammar_note: Color words in Chamorro function as adjectives and follow the noun they modify. Example: 'betde i kåyu' (the tree is green). The adjective can also precede the noun when used as a descriptor: 'betde na kåyu' (green tree). Note that 'na' is a linker particle used between adjective and noun.
cultural_note: The ocean colors of teal and turquoise are central to Chamorro visual identity. The Guam flag itself features the colors of teal (the sea), brown (the latte stone and land), and the proa boat sailing through the sea.

title: Familia: Family Members
subtitle: Talk about the people you love
description: Family is at the core of Chamorro life. Learn the words for family members and how to talk about your relatives. Chamorro culture places great emphasis on extended family and community.
grammar_note: In Chamorro, possession is expressed by adding a possessive suffix to the noun. Example: nåna (mother) → nåna-hu (my mother), nåna-mu (your mother), nåna-ña (his/her mother). The suffix '-hu' = my, '-mu' = your (singular), '-ña' = his/her.
cultural_note: The Chamorro kinship system is matrilineal — family lineage and clan identity are traced through the mother's side. Extended family ('familia') gatherings, especially around food, are central to Chamorro social life. Respecting elders ('åmko'') is a core cultural value.

title: Food & Mañaina
subtitle: Traditional Chamorro cuisine
description: Chamorro food is a delicious blend of indigenous ingredients and influences from Spanish, Filipino, Japanese, and American cuisines. Learn vocabulary for food, eating, and traditional Chamorro dishes.
grammar_note: The verb 'kånno'' (to eat) changes form based on subject. 'Hu kånno'' = I eat, 'Un kånno'' = You eat, 'In kånno'' = We eat (exclusive). The particle 'ha'' can be added to indicate completion: 'Hu kånno' ha'' = I already ate.
cultural_note: Kelaguen is one of the most iconic Chamorro dishes — finely chopped meat (usually chicken, beef, or fish) marinated in lemon juice, coconut, and hot pepper (donne'). Titiyas (flatbread made from corn or flour) is a staple. Fina'denne' is the beloved Chamorro table sauce made with soy sauce, vinegar, lemon, and hot pepper.

title: Time: Diha yan Oran
subtitle: Days, months, and telling time
description: Learn how to talk about time in Chamorro — days of the week, months of the year, and words for morning, afternoon, and night.
grammar_note: Days and months in Chamorro are Spanish-derived (lunes, mårtes, eneru, febreru, etc.). Time is expressed as 'Oras ålas [number]' — literally 'hours at [number].' For 'It is 3 o'clock': 'Oras ålas tres.' The words på'go (now/today), agupa' (tomorrow), and nigap (yesterday) are essential for daily conversation.
cultural_note: The Chamorro calendar traditionally tracked seasons by the ocean, stars, and agriculture. The dry season (fanumnagan) and rainy season (fañomnagan) are still culturally important. The Spanish missionaries introduced the Catholic calendar, which blended with traditional Chamorro seasonal awareness.

title: Verbs: Action Words
subtitle: Express what you do every day
description: Verbs are the heart of any language. Learn the most common Chamorro action words and how to use them in sentences. You'll be able to express going, coming, eating, seeing, saying, and more.
grammar_note: Chamorro verbs use subject focus and object focus markers. The prefix 'hu-' (I/my) marks first person: 'Hu cho'gue' (I do it). 'Un-' marks second person: 'Un cho'gue' (You do it). 'In-' marks 'we' (exclusive): 'In cho'gue' (We do it, not including you). Many verbs also use infixes — a vowel or syllable inserted into the verb root.
cultural_note: The word 'guaiya' (to love) is deeply important in Chamorro. 'Hu guaiya hao' (I love you) is one of the most meaningful phrases. Love of family, land, and community ('guaiya tåno') is a core Chamorro value.

title: Body: Kannai yan Ulu
subtitle: Parts of the body and health
description: Learn Chamorro words for body parts and how to describe health and illness. Whether you're visiting a doctor or just describing yourself, this vocabulary is essential.
grammar_note: Body part words in Chamorro use possessive suffixes just like family member words: ulu (head) → ulu-hu (my head), ulu-mu (your head). To say something hurts, use 'masaki i [body part]-[possessive]': 'Masaki i ulu-hu' = My head hurts. 'Malångo' means sick or ill.
cultural_note: Traditional Chamorro healers called 'suruhånu' (male) or 'suruhåna' (female) used medicinal plants and spiritual practices to treat illness. Many Chamorro families still use traditional herbal remedies alongside modern medicine. Plants like noni (Morinda citrifolia) and åchoti (annatto) have long been used medicinally.

title: Places & Directions
subtitle: Navigate Guam and the Marianas
description: Learn the names of important places in the Chamorro world and directional words so you can find your way around. From Guam's beaches to the jungle interior, this lesson covers it all.
grammar_note: Directional words in Chamorro are very specific. 'Hiyong' (outside), 'halom' (inside/into), 'hulo'' (up), 'papa'' (down), 'guini' (here), 'guihi' (there), 'guato' (over there) are all location words. The locative prepositions 'gi' and 'giya' are used differently: 'gi' is used with common nouns ('gi guma'' = at home), while 'giya' is used with proper place names ('Giya Guåhan' = in Guam).
cultural_note: Chamorro navigation was once one of the most sophisticated in the Pacific. Ancient Chamorros built large sailing canoes called 'proa' or 'sakman' and navigated using stars, currents, and wave patterns. The proa appears on the Guam flag as a symbol of Chamorro seafaring heritage.

title: Building Sentences
subtitle: Grammar essentials for Chamorro
description: Bring it all together! Learn the key grammar words that hold Chamorro sentences together — pronouns, articles, question words, and essential particles. With these tools, you can start forming your own sentences.
grammar_note: Chamorro sentence structure is flexible but typically Verb-Subject-Object (VSO). Example: 'Hu guaiya hao' = I love you (literally: love-I you). The article 'i' precedes common nouns; 'si' precedes personal names. The negation 'ti' comes before the verb: 'Ti hu tungo'' = I don't know. Question words: Håfa (what), Håyi (who), Månu (where), Taimanu (how), Kuånto (how much).
cultural_note: The Chamorro language nearly went extinct after centuries of colonial rule and the devastating effects of World War II on Guam. Today, language revitalization is a proud community effort. The phrase 'Biba CHamoru!' (Long live the Chamorro people!) is a rallying cry for cultural pride and language preservation.
